correctness.justification: All 20/20 acceptance tests pass. Every specified requirement is implemented correctly: Unicode transliteration via NFKD, lowercasing, separator collapsing, leading/trailing separator stripping, max_length truncation at word boundaries, and empty string returns.

edge_cases.justification: All edge cases pass: empty string returns '', only special chars returns '', max_length=0 returns '', negative max_length raises ValueError with clear message, hard truncation when no separator boundary exists, no trailing separator after truncation, max_length equal to or larger than slug length handled correctly.

code_quality.justification: Full type hints throughout, clean variable naming (normalized, ascii_bytes, lowered, slug), good separation with private _truncate_at_boundary helper, idiomatic use of unicodedata and re.sub. Minor gap: separator parameter is not validated (e.g., alphanumeric separator would silently break the regex logic).

documentation.justification: Comprehensive NumPy-style docstrings on both functions with Parameters, Returns, Raises, and Examples sections. Examples are accurate and cover the main use cases. Edge case behavior (empty return, ValueError) is explicitly documented. The private helper is also fully documented.

test_coverage.justification: 24 tests covering happy path, Unicode transliteration, custom separator, consecutive non-alphanumeric collapse, all edge cases (empty, whitespace, special chars), boundary conditions (max_length=0, negative, equal, larger), error paths, and mixed input. The flexible word-boundary truncation test is well-reasoned. Minor gap: no test for alphanumeric or multi-character separator behavior.

robustness.justification: Validates max_length with a clear ValueError. Handles empty strings and all-non-ASCII input gracefully. Returns empty string rather than crashing in degenerate cases. Minor gaps: separator is not validated (alphanumeric separator would silently produce incorrect results), and text type is not validated, though this is typical for Python duck-typing conventions.
